items.q: How does the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons depend on light intensity?
items.correct: It does not — max KE depends only on the frequency of the light; intensity affects the rate (number) of electrons emitted.
items.misconception: Higher intensity gives photoelectrons more kinetic energy.
items.random_wrong: Intensity has no effect on anything in the photoelectric effect.
items.why: Students confuse 'brighter light' with 'more energetic photons'; intensity gives more photons, but each one's energy is set by frequency.

items.q: In a balanced nuclear equation, what must be equal on both sides?
items.correct: The total mass number (top numbers) and the total atomic number (bottom numbers) must both balance.
items.misconception: Only the atomic number (proton count) needs to balance.
items.random_wrong: Only the total mass in kilograms needs to balance.
items.why: Students transfer 'balance the atoms' from chemical equations, but in nuclear equations identities can change while nucleon and charge counts are conserved.

items.q: What distinguishes gamma rays from X-rays of the same frequency?
items.correct: Origin — gamma rays come from nuclear processes; X-rays come from electron transitions or deceleration of charged particles.
items.misconception: Gamma rays have higher frequency than any X-ray.
items.random_wrong: Gamma rays are slower than X-rays.
items.why: Students distinguish bands by frequency exclusively, missing that gamma and X-ray bands overlap in frequency and are classified by origin.

items.q: Heisenberg's uncertainty principle was published in:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1927. “1913” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Maximum binding energy per nucleon occurs around mass number:
items.why: Correct answer: 56. “12” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Year Bohr published his hydrogen atom model?
items.why: Exact answer: 1913 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
